unit.name: Use theory of meaning and partial grounding
unit.description: A defense of the idea that meaning can come from use and from multiple forms of grounding, not only direct denotation.
unit.summary: Manning then contrasts denotational semantics with the use theory of meaning associated with Firth and later Wittgenstein, arguing that meaning can arise from how expressions are used rather than only from direct symbol-to-world denotation [ts=3421s] [ts=3532s]. He pushes back on the view that only explicit form-to-meaning mappings count as semantics, arguing instead that meanings are built through many kinds of connections—to things in the real world, to virtual worlds, and to other words in language [ts=3558s] [ts=3603s]. A key claim is that meaning is gradient rather than binary: we can understand a word more or less well [ts=3606s] [ts=3624s]. The shehnai example shows how pictures, sound, verbal explanation, and textual usage each provide different partial kinds of grounding, with corpus context revealing culturally important facets such as sound and association with weddings [ts=3631s] [ts=3815s].

key_points.text: The use theory of meaning treats meaning as arising from use rather than only from denotation.
Wittgenstein challenges the idea that natural language must be replaced by a different symbolic system to express meaning.
Meaning can be grounded through multiple connections: real-world reference, virtual worlds, and relations to other language.
Meaning is gradient rather than a 0/1 property.
The word 'shehnai' is used to show that pictures, sound, definitions, and contextual examples each contribute partial meaning.
Textual usage can reveal aspects of meaning, such as a shehnai's wailing sound and its connection to weddings, that a picture alone would miss.

unit_summary_for_generation: Manning then contrasts denotational semantics with the use theory of meaning associated with Firth and later Wittgenstein, arguing that meaning can arise from how expressions are used rather than only from direct symbol-to-world denotation [ts=3421s] [ts=3532s]. He pushes back on the view that only explicit form-to-meaning mappings count as semantics, arguing instead that meanings are built through many kinds of connections—to things in the real world, to virtual worlds, and to other words in language [ts=3558s] [ts=3603s]. A key claim is that meaning is gradient rather than binary: we can understand a word more or less well [ts=3606s] [ts=3624s]. The shehnai example shows how pictures, sound, verbal explanation, and textual usage each provide different partial kinds of grounding, with corpus context revealing culturally important facets such as sound and association with weddings [ts=3631s] [ts=3815s].

kp_catalog.name: Use theory and gradient grounding of meaning
kp_catalog.description: The idea that meaning can be partial, graded, and grounded through use, context, and multiple kinds of connection rather than only denotation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because use theory and gradient grounding of meaning synthesizes the idea that meaning can be partial, graded, and grounded through use, context, and multiple kinds of connection rather than only denotation into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Distributional semantics
kp_catalog.description: A word’s meaning is inferred from the contexts in which it appears, summarized by the slogan that a word is known by the company it keeps. The idea that linguistic representations gain meaning from the contexts in which words occur; presented as a central engine of modern NLP.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because distributional semantics formalizes how a word’s meaning is inferred from the contexts in which it appears, summarized by the slogan that a word is known by the company it keeps, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though importance labels split between critical and high.
